ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a term for water spinach as an ingredient (FOODON_03301710); no specific term exists for the stir-fried preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers the raw water spinach ingredient. No specific code exists for the stir-fried Burmese preparation; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried water spinach as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried water spinach is approximately 150 g, presented as a side dish alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Ipomoea aquatica (water spinach), cooked, stir-fried — FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked water spinach (FDC ID 168430) for cooking-oil fat adjustment
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw water spinach values from FAO/INFOODS SEA table adjusted for stir-frying with oil (approximately 5 g oil per 100 g cooked dish) using standard cooking-retention factors. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect fish sauce / oyster sauce seasoning typical of Burmese preparation. Values represent a plausible central estimate for restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of leafy green vegetables and stir-fried vegetable dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on cooked water spinach and comparable stir-fried leafy greens
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Burmese stir-fried water spinach found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with cooked leafy green vegetables (GI range 10–20, predominantly low) as documented in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.7) / 100 ≈ 1.3 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from fish sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 150 g restaurant serving may contribute approximately 630 mg sodium; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable to stay within the 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried water spinach has a low GI (approximately 15) and a glycemic load of approximately 1.3 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in potassium, folate, and vitamin A with moderate unsaturated fat from stir-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides beneficial micronutrients including potassium (312 mg/100 g) and folate (57 mcg/100 g) that support cardiovascular health; however, sodium content from seasoning sauces should be moderated in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried water spinach (ကန်စွန်းရွက်ကြော်, kan-swun-ywet kyaw) is a classic Burmese vegetable side dish made from Ipomoea aquatica — a semi-aquatic tropical plant known as water spinach, morning glory, or kangkung — cooked rapidly in a hot wok with garlic, cooking oil, and seasoned with fish sauce or oyster sauce, sometimes with fresh chilli. The dish is a staple across Myanmar and is widely served in Burmese restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. The leaves and tender stems are wilted by high-heat stir-frying, resulting in a dish with a slightly crisp texture, mild vegetal flavour, and a savoury, garlicky aroma. Nutritionally, stir-fried water spinach is low in calories (approximately 78 kcal per 100 g), provides modest protein (2.6 g), dietary fibre (2.1 g), and is a good source of vitamin A (180 mcg RAE), vitamin C (18 mg), folate (57 mcg), potassium (312 mg), and iron (1.7 mg). The fat content (5.2 g per 100 g) reflects the cooking oil used in stir-frying. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) from seasoning sauces, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-compatible when prepared with fish sauce rather than pork-derived products, and is dairy-free. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice or noodles in a Burmese meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Burmese fried water spinach?
Is kangkung goreng the same as Burmese water spinach stir-fry?
What is the GI of stir-fried morning glory?
Can diabetics eat stir-fried water spinach?
Is ကန်စွန်းရွက်ကြော် high in sodium?
What nutrients are in water spinach?

water spinach: ကန်စွန်းရွက်

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Burmese stir-fried water spinach was found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA raw water spinach data with standard stir-frying oil and sodium adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable leafy green vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient (Ipomoea aquatica) is well-documented in approved sources and the cooking method adjustments are standard and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Vegetables' — water spinach is a leafy vegetable, not a grain or starch. 2. Cuisine tags 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' in the input appear to be field survey platform artefacts and do not apply to this dish; they have been discarded. 3. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; an exact laboratory analysis of the Burmese restaurant preparation would improve confidence to high. 4. Sodium value (420 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant seasoning with fish sauce or oyster sauce; home-prepared versions with less sauce would have lower sodium. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in plant-based stir-fried dishes per FAO/INFOODS data. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12 (fish sauce contributes negligible B12 at typical usage levels); confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS plant food data. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as water spinach and cooking oil do not contribute meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS and USDA data. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish is plant-based; fish sauce contributes negligible cholesterol at typical usage. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.9), potassium_mg (312.0), calcium_mg (77.0), iron_mg (1.7), cholesterol_mg (0.0), vitamin_c_mg (18.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (180.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.10), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.9), vitamin_b6_mg (0.10), folate_mcg_dfe (57.0), vitamin_e_mg (1.1), phosphorus_mg (48.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (0.3), selenium_mcg (1.2), water_g (84.0) are all estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA cooked water spinach data. 10. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this specific Burmese preparation exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 11. Burmese cuisine does not require Tamil or Hindi local names under the multilingual rules (those apply to Singaporean dishes); local_names.tamil and local_names.hindi are null accordingly. 12. aliases_by_language consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language also appear in the flat aliases list.
